summary:
The bill, titled the "Kids Online Safety and Privacy Act," aims to protect the safety and privacy of children and teens on the internet. 

Title I establishes a "duty of care" for covered platforms—such as online platforms, video games, and streaming services used by minors—requiring them to prevent and mitigate harms including mental health disorders, addiction-like behaviors, online bullying, sexual exploitation, and predatory marketing. Platforms must provide safeguards for minors, such as limiting communication, restricting data sharing, controlling addictive design features like infinite scrolling, and allowing users to opt out of personalized recommendation systems. Platforms must also offer parental tools, default to the most protective settings for minors, and provide reporting mechanisms for harms. Large platforms are subject to annual independent, third-party audits and public transparency reports. Additionally, platforms using opaque algorithms must allow users to switch to an input-transparent algorithm that does not use user-specific data unless expressly provided. Title I takes effect 18 months after enactment, with the algorithm provisions taking effect after one year.

Title II amends the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) to extend privacy protections to teens (individuals aged 13 to 16). It prohibits the collection, use, or disclosure of personal information for individual-specific advertising to children and teens, requires operators to provide data deletion and correction options, and mandates reasonable data security practices. 

Title III requires federal agencies to include a list of outdated or duplicative recurring reports in their annual budget justifications and recommend whether to sunset, modify, or consolidate them. 

The bill exempts certain entities, such as common carriers, email services, schools, libraries, and nonprofits, from the covered platform requirements. It does not preempt state laws that provide greater protections to minors and does not alter Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act. The bill also establishes a Kids Online Safety Council to advise on online safety matters and directs studies on social media harms, age verification systems, and teen privacy in financial technology products.
